text: Hi, Dr. Andrea Vidali here, and I'm presenting an example of an operation for endometriosis, quite advanced, probably somewhere between stage three and four. This is the first operation. The video is quite a bit sped up, obviously, so that I could go over it with you guys without boring you too much. With all endometriosis cases, the methodology, the technique is always standardized. As usual, as I always say, always go from known to unknown. Start at the top of the pelvis, identify your ureters, work your way down the sides, both left and right until you find the right spaces, identify the ureters, dissect them off. until you get to the center and then address whatever is going on in the middle in terms of rectal disease or posterior cervical disease, at which point then you can proceed with the rest of the operation. The goal of the surgery as usual is always to excise and not to leave any disease behind whenever possible.

The surgeon identifies the left ovary, visible as a shiny white structure on the right side of the screen, adherent to the posterior aspect of the uterus. The bowel is being medialized to clear the surgical field. An epiploic appendix with disease was removed earlier. Various colors of endometriosis are noted, including red, white, and black, with brownish areas indicating old blood.

The surgeon uses fluorescence to identify the ureter, facilitating the procedure. A cyst is drained to prevent the spread of chocolate fluid, causing the ovary to elevate slightly. As the cyst deflates, the rectum is visibly pulled towards the posterior aspect of the uterus. The ovary is gently elevated, and a temporary ovarian suspension is performed using a V-lock suture to free the assistant for other tasks.

The surgeon accidentally cuts the V-lock suture but ties a knot to secure it. The ovary is elevated, allowing the surgeon to proceed with dissection along the left pelvic sidewall. Using robotic surgery, the surgeon employs the right hand for medial traction and the left hand with scissors for lateral dissection, optimizing traction-countertraction. The scissors are used closed to navigate small spaces, similar to a hook, while dissecting along the peritoneum towards the ovarian fossa, guided by fluorescence to identify the ureter.

The surgeon uses a grasper to pull tissue medially, creating space on both sides to access the rectovaginal space. The dissection focuses on detaching the sigmoid colon from the posterior aspect of the uterus, with visible endometriosis nodules present on the rectum. The goal is to excise these nodules to prevent future adhesion formation.

The surgeon switches instruments and begins dissection by opening the peritoneum to access the retroperitoneal space. The dissection progresses from the utero-ovarian vessels downward, with the rectum visibly adhered to the posterior aspect of the uterus.

The surgeon carefully dissects the avascular space between the rectum and the posterior aspect of the uterus, using monopolar cautery in short bursts to minimize thermal damage. The peritoneum with endometriosis is removed close to the rectal serosa, ensuring the rectum falls into its normal position. The surgeon is cautious to avoid entering the vagina or rectum during the dissection.

The surgeon uses a VALCHA uterine retractor to expose the cervix, revealing the back of the uterus, which is now clean of disease. The surgeon performs additional excision of small areas of remaining endometriosis, restoring normal pelvic anatomy. The rectum is observed to have returned to its natural position after the excision of a large area of peritoneum.

The surgeon pulls on the rectum where a nodule is located, freeing fibrotic endometriosis and expanding the rectovaginal space. The lesion is excised without entering the rectum, using anterior rectal shaving to remove serosal and muscularis lesions. Brown areas on the appendix are noted, which are not endometriosis but hemocedrine-laden macrophages, and are removed.

And actually, on the path report, whenever you see hemocedrine-laden macrophages, Although it is associated with endometriosis, I always like to see glands to really know factually that the endometriosis was taken. If I only see hemocetarine-laden macrophages, I feel, well, is there endometriosis somewhere else? So just for people to know.

The surgeon carefully performs a cystectomy, meticulously peeling off the endometriotic cysts from the ovary to avoid any thermal or physical damage, preserving the patient's fertility. An appendectomy is also performed during this stage of the surgery.
